steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: biliary-duct obstruction/choledocholithiasis, hepatic pathology (given transaminase elevation), or other intra-abdominal process given 6-week subacute course and fever
ensemble.information_gap: The anatomic source and severity of the intra-abdominal process driving the markedly elevated lipase, transaminase elevation, fever, and 6-week pain course cannot be determined from labs and chest X-ray alone — specifically whether there is pancreatic inflammation/necrosis, biliary ductal dilation, hepatic pathology, or an alternative abdominal source
ensemble.expected_finding: Peripancreatic fat stranding or pancreatic edema/necrosis consistent with acute pancreatitis, and/or common bile duct dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis as a precipitating cause; alternatively, hepatic, colonic (diverticular), or other intra-abdominal pathology explaining the fever and subacute course
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: Lipase is markedly elevated at 489 IU/L (>8× upper limit of normal), transaminases are significantly elevated (ALT 154, AST 102, ALP 220), the outpatient RUQ ultrasound was negative for cholelithiasis, and the patient now has fever and worsening pain over 6 weeks — CT with contrast is the appropriate next step to characterize pancreatic and biliary anatomy, assess for complications, and identify any alternative or concurrent abdominal pathology.
grounding: History: outpatient RUQ US which was negative for any acute findings or evidence of cholelithiasis
History: waxing and waning abdominal pain in the epigastrium over the past 6 weeks associated with intermittent nausea and vomiting
History: developed a fever to 102 ___ evening
Prior imaging: No evidence of free air is seen beneath the diaphragm
ensemble.reasoning: With a lipase nearly 8× the upper limit of normal, significant transaminase and alkaline phosphatase elevations, a prior negative RUQ ultrasound, and now a fever after 6 weeks of episodic epigastric pain, I need cross-sectional imaging to determine whether this is acute pancreatitis (and if so, assess for necrosis or complications), whether there is biliary ductal dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis missed on ultrasound, or whether another intra-abdominal process (hepatic, colonic given known diverticulosis) is contributing. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the most efficient single test to answer all of these questions simultaneously.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: biliary-duct obstruction/choledocholithiasis, hepatic pathology (given transaminase elevation), or other intra-abdominal process given 6-week subacute course and fever
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The anatomic source and severity of the intra-abdominal process driving the markedly elevated lipase, transaminase elevation, fever, and 6-week pain course cannot be determined from labs and chest X-ray alone — specifically whether there is pancreatic inflammation/necrosis, biliary ductal dilation, hepatic pathology, or an alternative abdominal source
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Peripancreatic fat stranding or pancreatic edema/necrosis consistent with acute pancreatitis, and/or common bile duct dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis as a precipitating cause; alternatively, hepatic, colonic (diverticular), or other intra-abdominal pathology explaining the fever and subacute course
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: Lipase is markedly elevated at 489 IU/L (>8× upper limit of normal), transaminases are significantly elevated (ALT 154, AST 102, ALP 220), the outpatient RUQ ultrasound was negative for cholelithiasis, and the patient now has fever and worsening pain over 6 weeks — CT with contrast is the appropriate next step to characterize pancreatic and biliary anatomy, assess for complications, and identify any alternative or concurrent abdominal pathology.
grounding: History: outpatient RUQ US which was negative for any acute findings or evidence of cholelithiasis
History: waxing and waning abdominal pain in the epigastrium over the past 6 weeks associated with intermittent nausea and vomiting
History: developed a fever to 102 ___ evening
Prior imaging: No evidence of free air is seen beneath the diaphragm
representative_ex_ante.reasoning: With a lipase nearly 8× the upper limit of normal, significant transaminase and alkaline phosphatase elevations, a prior negative RUQ ultrasound, and now a fever after 6 weeks of episodic epigastric pain, I need cross-sectional imaging to determine whether this is acute pancreatitis (and if so, assess for necrosis or complications), whether there is biliary ductal dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis missed on ultrasound, or whether another intra-abdominal process (hepatic, colonic given known diverticulosis) is contributing. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the most efficient single test to answer all of these questions simultaneously.

vindication.actual_finding: The CT showed a normally enhancing pancreas without peripancreatic fat stranding or necrosis, no biliary ductal dilation, a normal gallbladder, no hepatic pathology beyond mild steatosis, no diverticulitis, and no other acute intra-abdominal process to explain the clinical presentation.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The expected findings of pancreatic inflammation/necrosis, biliary ductal dilation, or an alternative acute intra-abdominal source were all absent, leaving the markedly elevated lipase, transaminase elevation, fever, and subacute pain course unexplained by imaging.

steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: biliary pathology (choledocholithiasis, biliary sludge, sphincter of Oddi dysfunction, or periampullary pathology) given elevated liver enzymes (ALT 154, AST 102, ALP 220) with markedly elevated lipase (489) and a periampullary duodenal diverticulum noted on CT
ensemble.information_gap: The CT showed no biliary ductal dilation and a normal-appearing gallbladder, but ultrasound provides superior real-time assessment of the gallbladder wall, pericholecystic fluid, gallstones/sludge, and common bile duct diameter that CT may miss — particularly given the elevated liver enzymes and lipase suggesting a biliary etiology for pancreatitis that CT did not fully characterize
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallstones, biliary sludge, gallbladder wall thickening/pericholecystic fluid (acute cholecystitis), or common bile duct dilation suggesting choledocholithiasis as the trigger for the elevated liver enzymes and lipase
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: Despite a negative outpatient RUQ US and a CT showing no ductal dilation, the combination of markedly elevated lipase (489), transaminase elevation (ALT 154, AST 102), elevated ALP (220), and a periampullary duodenal diverticulum on CT creates a genuine information gap that a dedicated liver/gallbladder ultrasound — with its superior sensitivity for gallstones and sludge — is well-positioned to address.
grounding: CT ABDOMEN: 'The gallbladder appears normal. No intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation.'
CT ABDOMEN: 'There is a periampullary duodenal diverticulum incidentally noted.'
History: 'outpatient RUQ US which was negative for any acute findings or evidence of cholelithiasis'
History: 'waxing and waning abdominal pain in the epigastrium over the past 6 weeks associated with intermittent nausea and vomiting'
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has biochemical evidence of pancreatitis (lipase 489) with a hepatocellular and cholestatic liver enzyme pattern (ALT 154, AST 102, ALP 220) pointing strongly toward a biliary etiology, yet the CT did not demonstrate ductal dilation or obvious gallstones. Ultrasound is more sensitive than CT for detecting gallstones and biliary sludge, and can provide real-time assessment of the gallbladder wall and common bile duct diameter that may have been missed on CT — particularly relevant given the periampullary diverticulum noted on CT, which can be associated with biliary dysfunction. The prior outpatient RUQ US was negative, but the clinical picture has evolved with new fever and worsening pain, making repeat dedicated biliary ultrasound a rational next step to identify or exclude a biliary source driving the pancreatitis.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: biliary pathology (choledocholithiasis, biliary sludge, sphincter of Oddi dysfunction, or periampullary pathology) given elevated liver enzymes (ALT 154, AST 102, ALP 220) with markedly elevated lipase (489) and a periampullary duodenal diverticulum noted on CT

vindication.actual_finding: Ultrasound identified a 0.4 cm gallstone and a 0.5 cm gallbladder polyp, with a normal common hepatic duct (4 mm) and no evidence of acute cholecystitis or biliary dilation.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The finding of a gallstone partially confirms the expected biliary etiology (cholelithiasis present), though the absence of cholecystitis, biliary sludge, or CBD dilation limits the explanation for the elevated liver enzymes and lipase to a passed stone or microlithiasis rather than active obstruction or cholecystitis.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis or biliary-duct obstruction (common bile duct stone, ampullary pathology, or periampullary process)
ensemble.information_gap: Whether there is a common bile duct stone, ampullary lesion, or other biliary/pancreatic ductal pathology that explains the elevated lipase (489 IU/L), transaminitis (ALT 154, AST 102, ALP 220), and recurrent epigastric pain despite a CT showing no pancreatic necrosis and no ductal dilation on ultrasound
ensemble.expected_finding: A choledocholithiasis (CBD stone), biliary sludge, or periampullary abnormality causing partial obstruction at the level of the ampulla of Vater — or alternatively, subtle pancreatic ductal dilation or disruption not visible on CT/US
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: MRCP is the appropriate next step to evaluate the biliary and pancreatic ductal anatomy in detail given markedly elevated lipase and cholestatic liver enzymes with a periampullary duodenal diverticulum noted on CT, a gallstone on repeat ultrasound, and no ductal dilation seen on prior imaging — CT and US have limited sensitivity for small CBD stones and subtle ductal pathology.
grounding: Prior imaging 2 (CT): There is a periampullary duodenal diverticulum incidentally noted.
Prior imaging 2 (CT): No intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation. The gallbladder appears normal. The pancreas enhances normally.
Prior imaging 3 (US): A gallstone measures approximately 0.4 cm. There is no evidence of acute cholecystitis. CHD: 4 mm.
History: Pt has had waxing and waning abdominal pain in the epigastrium over the past 6 weeks associated with intermittent nausea and vomiting.
History: outpatient RUQ US which was negative for any acute findings or evidence of cholelithiasis (prior US negative, but current US now shows gallstone)
ensemble.reasoning: The patient has a markedly elevated lipase consistent with pancreatitis, combined with a cholestatic liver enzyme pattern (elevated ALT, AST, ALP with normal bilirubin), a newly identified gallstone on repeat ultrasound, and a periampullary duodenal diverticulum on CT — all pointing toward a biliary etiology. However, CT and ultrasound have limited sensitivity for small common bile duct stones and subtle ductal pathology, and the CBD measures only 4 mm (not clearly dilated). MRCP without contrast is the ideal non-invasive next step to directly visualize the biliary and pancreatic ducts for choledocholithiasis, ampullary pathology, or ductal disruption that would explain the recurrent, now-worsening presentation.

vindication.actual_finding: MRI showed no choledocholithiasis, no significant stricture, no pancreatic ductal abnormality, and an unremarkable pancreas, with only gallbladder sludge and minimal biliary ductal dilatation up to the ampulla without a focal lesion.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The expected CBD stone, biliary sludge obstruction, periampullary lesion, or pancreatic ductal disruption was not found; the imaging did not identify a structural cause for the elevated lipase and transaminitis.
